original_abstract:
Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have emerged as transitionary technologies with immense potential in civilian and public sectors, particularly for missions that jeopardize human safety. While single-UAV operations are common, multi-UAV systems offer superior efficiency and economic advantages through collaborative task execution. However, creating stable, reliable, and context-aware networks for these fleets remains a significant hurdle. 

Current research often relies on frameworks developed for Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs) and Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs), yet these models fail to account for the unique constraints of the aerial domain. UAV networks are characterized by highly fluid topologies, fluctuating levels of dynamism, and frequent link intermittency. While ad hoc mesh architectures are theoretically ideal, the specific structural requirements of multi-UAV communication remain under-explored. Furthermore, the integration of Software Defined Networking (SDN) presents a promising path for flexible deployment, enhanced security, and centralized management, yet its application in this space is still in its infancy.

Routing in UAV networks is exceptionally demanding, requiring protocols that can adapt to rapid mobility, unpredictable partitioning, and varying link quality while operating under strict power constraints. Because drones may fail or drift, ensuring delay and disruption tolerance is a critical design necessity. Additionally, the limited operational lifespan of individual nodes necessitates seamless handover mechanisms to maintain connectivity—an area where the research community has yet to reach a consensus. Finally, as UAVs are inherently energy-constrained, there is an urgent need for "green" protocols across all network layers to maximize mission duration. This article provides a comprehensive survey of the state-of-the-art research addressing these challenges, aiming to identify existing gaps and catalyze further innovation in this specialized class of networks.
humanized_abstract:
UAVs can be seen as transition technology with enormous application potential in the civilian/public domain for dangerous operations. While the majority of UAV operations still happen as single-UAV missions, multiple-UAV operation will not only improve the operational performance, but also become economically more efficient. A further challenge in modern multi-UAV operations is the establishment of a stable reliable ad-hoc communication network (sensing the environment and adapting to it) that supports operation safety and performance.

Existing research mostly uses mobility models and network architecture frameworks derived from other mobile ad hoc network (MANET) and vehicular ad hoc networks (VANET) areas, which are often not appropriate for the aerial wireless channel. The UAV networks generally exhibit highly dynamic network topology, varying degrees of network mobility and link unpredictable behavior. Although ad hoc mesh networking is considered one of the most appropriate network architectures for UAVs due to its ability to provide seamless and continuous connectivity and high communication reliability; the optimization of the structural elements for multi-UAV communication has not been deeply studied. Also, leveraging Software Defined Networking (SDN) in UAV networks can enable many attractive benefits including rapid network deployment, improved security features, and full centralised control of the network functionalities, but still, it is an unexplored area of research for UAV networks.

One of the major challenges in designing networks of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) is the routing process. Due to high speeds of the UAVs, possible network partitions, wireless channel degradation, energy constraints, etc., designing an efficient routing protocol for the network becomes highly challenging. Node failures or losing track of the UAVs is highly expected. Thus, designing a delay and disruption tolerant network (DTN) is a must for UAV networks. Another crucial challenge is the limited lifetime of a UAV. The handover process from one point to another has not been standardized in the networking community. So, the energy efficiency of all the layers of communication protocols for the UAV networks is also required to enhance the life time of the UAS missions. This survey article covers the recent works on designing efficient routing protocol for UAV networks and thereby understanding the existing challenges and their corresponding solutions, which helps in filling up existing research gaps.
